Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A person, team, facility, equipment, material, or other asset used to complete project work.
Expansion: In PMI usage, a resource is anything needed to perform project work, not just people. It includes human capability as well as equipment, facilities, materials, funding support, and other assets that enable delivery. Because projects depend on finite resources, planning and managing them is central to estimating, scheduling, cost control, and execution.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A resource is any asset applied to project activities or work packages to produce deliverables. PMI uses the term broadly so teams do not treat staffing as the only constraint that matters.

Why it matters and where it applies: resource availability shapes schedule realism, cost estimates, sequencing decisions, and delivery risk. It appears across planning and execution in staffing, equipment booking, material supply, and access to specialist capability.

The most common misconception: that resource means only a named person or role on the team. In practice, critical resources often include scarce environments, machinery, funding, data, or physical space, and these can constrain delivery as much as labour does.

How it shows up in real projects: teams estimate resource requirements, acquire people and assets, track utilisation, resolve conflicts, and adjust plans when key resources are overallocated, delayed, or unavailable.
Contrast: Resource assignment — a resource is the asset itself; resource assignment is the allocation of that asset to specific work.
Example: A software project uses developers, testers, a cloud test environment, and a cybersecurity lab as project resources needed to deliver the release.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — resource; A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, exact page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: The assignment of available resources to specific project activities, work packages, or time periods.
Expansion: Resource allocation matches people, equipment, facilities, funding, or materials to the work that needs to be performed. It supports realistic planning by making visible who or what is needed, when it is needed, and where constraints may affect delivery. In PMI practice, it is closely tied to activity resource estimating, schedule development, and ongoing monitoring of resource use.
Contrast: Resource optimisation — allocation assigns resources to work, while optimisation adjusts the plan to use those resources more effectively within constraints.
Example: A construction project manager assigns two electricians and one inspection window to the wiring activities scheduled for the third week of site execution.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The allocation of a resource to a specific project activity or work package.
Expansion: Resource assignment connects available resources to the work they will perform. It supports accountability, scheduling, and workload balancing by showing who or what is committed to each task. PMI uses it to make execution responsibilities explicit and to support schedule and resource management.
Contrast: Responsibility assignment matrix — resource assignment allocates resources to work; a responsibility assignment matrix maps deliverables or activities to levels of responsibility across roles.
Example: A project manager assigns a database specialist and a staging server to the data migration work package during the final two weeks of system testing.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — resource assignment; A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, exact page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: The extent to which needed resources are obtainable and ready for use when project work requires them.
Expansion: Resource availability affects whether activities can start and finish as planned because people, equipment, materials, facilities, or funding may be constrained. In PMI scheduling and planning practice, it is a key input to estimating durations, sequencing work, and developing realistic schedules. Limited availability often drives trade-offs among scope, timing, cost, and quality.
Contrast: Resource calendar — resource availability is the actual condition of a resource being obtainable when needed, while a resource calendar records the times when that resource is expected to be available.
Example: A construction project pushes back commissioning because the specialist inspection team is not available during the original handover window.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, availability of resources, pages 66 and 120

Core Meaning: A hierarchical representation of resources organized by category and type.
Expansion: A Resource Breakdown Structure groups project resources into logical categories such as people, equipment, materials, or facilities. It helps the team understand resource classes, assess capacity needs, and support planning and control. PMI uses it as a way to structure resource information distinctly from the work itself.
Contrast: Work Breakdown Structure — a Resource Breakdown Structure organizes resources by type or category; a WBS organizes deliverables and the work needed to create them.
Example: An infrastructure project structures resources into civil engineers, site supervisors, heavy machinery, concrete materials, and testing facilities before detailed assignment planning.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, 9.2.1.3 Resource Breakdown Structure, p. 323

Core Meaning: A calendar that identifies the working days and shifts on which a resource is available.
Expansion: A resource calendar shows when a person, team, or physical resource can work on the project. It is used to align scheduling with actual availability, including holidays, shifts, and time off. PMI treats it as a scheduling input that helps prevent unrealistic assignments and date conflicts.
Contrast: Project calendar — a resource calendar shows availability for a specific resource; a project calendar shows the working and nonworking time for the project as a whole.
Example: A project schedules a specialist only on weekdays because the resource calendar shows that the person is unavailable on weekends.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition / PMI Lexicon — Resource calendar

Core Meaning: A limitation on the availability, capacity, timing, or type of resources that restricts how project work can be planned or executed.
Expansion: A resource constraint exists when needed people, equipment, materials, funding, or facilities are not available in the amount or timeframe the project would ideally require. It affects sequencing, duration, cost, and delivery choices because work must be adapted to finite capacity. In practice, resource constraints are among the most common drivers of schedule trade-offs and prioritisation decisions.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A resource constraint is any cap or shortage that limits the project's ability to use resources exactly when and how it wants. The constraint may involve quantity, skills, timing, location, or competing demand from other work.

Why it matters and where it applies: resource constraints directly shape schedules, budgets, and delivery strategies across predictive, agile, and hybrid environments. They are especially important in matrix organisations, capital projects, and multi-project portfolios where the same critical resources are shared.

The most common misconception: that a resource constraint is only a staffing problem. In reality, constrained resources can include specialist equipment, scarce materials, approval capacity, funding windows, or access to key stakeholders.

How it shows up in real projects: teams delay tasks until a specialist becomes available, level workloads to avoid over-allocation, split releases to fit funding limits, or re-plan dependencies because a shared test environment cannot support concurrent work.
Contrast: Constraint — a resource constraint is a specific limitation tied to resource availability or capacity, while a constraint can be any limiting factor such as scope, schedule, cost, quality, or regulation.
Example: An ERP implementation must stagger configuration and testing because the same database architect is assigned part-time across three concurrent projects.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Seventh Edition, page 264

Core Meaning: A bar chart that displays the amount of a resource scheduled to work over a series of time periods.
Expansion: A resource histogram shows resource allocation over time, with bars representing planned usage per period and often a line marking maximum availability. It helps identify over-allocation and supports resource leveling and smoothing decisions.
Contrast: Gantt chart - a histogram shows resource quantity over time, while a Gantt chart shows activity timing and duration.
Example: A histogram reveals a tester is booked 60 hours in a 40-hour week, signaling over-allocation.
Source: PMBOK Guide - resource histogram

Core Meaning: A resource optimization technique in which adjustments are made to the project schedule to optimize the allocation of resources and which may affect the critical path.
Expansion: Resource leveling adjusts activity dates to resolve over-allocation and align the schedule with actual resource availability. Unlike techniques that preserve planned dates where possible, it may extend durations and change the critical path if constrained resources cannot support the original sequence. PMI treats it as a practical scheduling response when resources are limited or fixed and schedule realism matters more than protecting the initial timeline.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Resource leveling is a schedule adjustment technique used when key people, equipment, or materials are overcommitted. The project manager shifts activities within or beyond float, or even delays work, so resource demand better matches actual capacity.

Why it matters and where it applies: plans often look feasible until the same specialist, machine, or crew is assigned to overlapping tasks. Resource leveling matters in projects with scarce expertise, shared resources across multiple projects, or hard staffing limits, because it produces a schedule that can actually be executed.

The most common misconception: that resource leveling is the same as smoothing and should not change end dates. In fact, leveling is specifically used when resource limits drive the schedule, so finish dates and the critical path may change.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager may delay noncritical engineering tasks because the same senior architect is also needed on a regulatory design review. The updated schedule becomes longer, but it reflects real capacity instead of an impossible staffing plan.
Contrast: Resource smoothing — resource leveling resolves over-allocation even if it changes the critical path or finish date; resource smoothing adjusts within existing schedule limits where possible.
Example: A construction project postpones some interior work by two weeks because the only certified electrical crew is already committed to critical installation tasks on another building area.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition — Resource leveling, p. 215

Core Meaning: The processes used to identify, acquire, manage, and control the resources needed for the project.
Expansion: Resource management covers how a project plans for and uses people, equipment, materials, facilities, and other needed resources. PMI treats it as a full management domain that includes estimating needs, acquiring resources, assigning them, developing team capability, and monitoring utilisation. It matters because resource limits often shape schedule, cost, feasibility, and risk. In practice, it connects planning decisions to day-to-day execution so the right resources are available at the right time.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Resource management is the coordinated planning and control of all resources required to deliver project work. It includes identifying what is needed, securing it, allocating it, and adjusting use as conditions change.

Why it matters and where it applies: most projects are constrained by scarce skills, shared teams, specialist equipment, or material availability. Effective resource management helps prevent bottlenecks, supports realistic schedules, and improves the chance that commitments can actually be met.

The most common misconception: that resource management only means assigning people to tasks. In PMI terms it also covers physical resources, acquisition timing, capacity constraints, team development, and ongoing control of resource use.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager negotiates for specialist engineers, sequences use of shared test equipment, updates resource calendars, and resolves conflicts when multiple projects need the same capability at once.
Contrast: Resource calendar — resource management is the overall discipline of planning and controlling resources; a resource calendar only shows when a specific resource is available.
Example: A construction project coordinates trades, cranes, and concrete deliveries so each required resource is available in sequence without delaying critical work.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition — Project Resource Management, pp. 307-362

Core Meaning: A component of the project management plan that describes how project resources are acquired, allocated, managed, and controlled.
Expansion: The resource management plan sets out the approach for planning, acquiring, managing, and releasing team and physical resources. It typically addresses roles and responsibilities, organisational charts, resource calendars, training, recognition, and compliance considerations. In PMI practice it provides the rules and structure for managing the resources needed to deliver the project.
Contrast: Resource requirements — the resource management plan explains how resources will be managed overall; resource requirements specify the types and quantities needed for particular work.
Example: On a hospital expansion project, the resource management plan defines specialist trade roles, equipment booking rules, onboarding, shift constraints, and how scarce crane time will be scheduled.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 9.1.3.1, p. 318

Core Meaning: An individual with management authority over one or more resources used by the project.
Expansion: A resource manager, often a functional or line manager, owns the people or physical resources the project needs and negotiates their availability with the project manager. In matrix organizations this shared authority makes the relationship between the PM and resource managers critical to securing committed resources.
Contrast: Project manager - the PM directs the project work, while the resource manager controls the supply of the resources that perform it.
Example: A functional engineering manager who lends two developers to a project is acting as their resource manager.
Source: PMBOK Guide - functional / resource manager role

Core Meaning: The adjustment of activity dates, sequencing, or assignments to use available resources as effectively as possible within project constraints.
Expansion: Resource optimisation is a scheduling and planning concept used to balance resource demand with resource availability. PMI commonly discusses it through techniques such as resource levelling and resource smoothing, which change the schedule or assignment pattern to reduce overloads and improve feasibility. It matters when the initial plan exceeds available capacity or creates inefficient usage patterns.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Resource optimisation is the deliberate reshaping of planned work so the project uses limited resources in a practical, sustainable way. It usually involves changing timing, sequencing, or assignment patterns rather than changing project objectives.

Why it matters and where it applies: most projects face shared specialists, limited equipment, budget ceilings, or hard calendar constraints. Optimisation helps produce schedules that can actually be executed, especially in matrix organisations, construction, engineering, and multi-project environments.

The most common misconception: that resource optimisation always means making people fully utilised at every moment. In practice, the goal is not constant utilisation but a workable plan that respects constraints, reduces harmful overloads, and supports delivery performance.

How it shows up in real projects: a scheduler delays noncritical tasks to avoid overbooking a testing team, or shifts work within float so a scarce subject-matter expert can support multiple deliverables without causing repeated bottlenecks.
Contrast: Resource allocation — allocation places resources onto work, while optimisation refines that plan to resolve conflicts and improve feasibility.
Example: In an IT rollout, the project manager shifts several training tasks by one week because the same network engineer was assigned to three parallel activities on the same days.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 6.5.2.3 and Section 6.6.2.4

Core Meaning: A scheduling technique used to adjust activity start and finish dates so resource usage fits resource constraints or balances demand.
Expansion: Resource optimization is the use of scheduling methods such as resource leveling and resource smoothing to create a more achievable schedule. PMI treats it as a way to resolve overallocations, capacity limits, and uneven workloads that make a schedule unrealistic. It is foundational because even well-defined scope and sequencing can fail if the plan ignores actual resource availability.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Resource optimization is the deliberate adjustment of a schedule so the planned work better matches available people and physical resources. It is most often achieved through two techniques:

- Resource leveling: used when resource limits drive the schedule, so it can change the critical path and end date.
- Resource smoothing: stays within float and tries to preserve the completion date.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects rarely have unlimited access to specialist staff, equipment, facilities, or environments. Optimization makes schedules executable by reducing overload, avoiding impossible overlaps, and improving the reliability of commitments in construction, technology, engineering, operations, and other delivery settings.

The most common misconception: that resource optimization is just about making people busy all the time. In practice, its purpose is not maximum utilisation for its own sake but a feasible flow of work that respects constraints, protects delivery dates where possible, and reduces burnout and conflict.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager shifts testing work because the same test environment is booked by multiple teams, delays a task until a certified engineer is available, or smooths design work across weeks so the core team is not overloaded in one period and idle in the next.
Contrast: Resource planning — resource planning determines what resources are needed; resource optimization adjusts the schedule to use those resources realistically.
Example: In an ERP rollout, the schedule is reworked because the same data-migration specialist was assigned to three parallel activities, so tasks are leveled across two additional weeks.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 6.5.2.3, p. 191

Core Meaning: Scheduling techniques that adjust the schedule based on resource supply and demand, including resource leveling and resource smoothing.
Expansion: Resource optimization techniques modify the project schedule to address resource availability constraints. Resource leveling adjusts start and finish dates to balance demand with supply and can change the critical path and the project end date. Resource smoothing adjusts activities only within their available float, so it does not delay completion but may not fully resolve over-allocation.
Contrast: Resource leveling vs resource smoothing - leveling can extend the project end date to honor resource limits, while smoothing stays within float and protects the end date.
Example: A planner levels resources to avoid double-booking an engineer, accepting a one-week slip.
Source: PMBOK Guide - resource optimization techniques

Core Meaning: The process of determining what resources are needed for project work and when they will be required.
Expansion: Resource planning is the practical activity of identifying required people, equipment, materials, facilities, and related capacity over time. In PMI terms, this idea is expressed through estimating activity resources and planning resource management rather than as a single formal glossary entry. It is widely used in practice to align demand, availability, and delivery commitments before execution begins.
Contrast: Resource management plan — resource planning determines needed resources and timing; the resource management plan documents the policies and approach for managing them.
Example: A manufacturing-automation project forecasts when electricians, PLC engineers, test rigs, and commissioning windows will be needed for each phase of the implementation.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The types and quantities of resources needed to complete project work.
Expansion: Resource requirements describe what resources are needed, in what amount, and sometimes when they are needed. They support planning, estimating, and acquisition decisions. PMI uses them to translate scope and schedule needs into staffing, equipment, and material demands.
Contrast: Activity duration estimates — resource requirements define what resources are needed; activity duration estimates define how long the work is expected to take.
Example: A project plan states that two testers, one business analyst, and a dedicated test environment are required for the final release cycle.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Resource requirements

Core Meaning: A schedule optimization technique in which activities are adjusted within their float so resource demand is kept at a more even level.
Expansion: Resource smoothing shifts work within available float to reduce extreme peaks and valleys in resource usage. It aims to make utilization more stable without changing the project end date or violating major schedule constraints. PMI treats it as a distinct optimization technique from resource leveling because it works within existing timing limits.
Contrast: Resource leveling — resource smoothing uses available float and preserves the planned finish date; resource leveling may delay activities and change the critical path.
Example: A project manager moves noncritical training tasks by a few days so the same specialist is not double-booked during the busiest week.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition — Resource smoothing, p. 194

Core Meaning: The amount of a resource's capacity assigned to an activity, usually expressed as a percentage or similar measure of availability.
Expansion: Resource units indicate how much of a person, team, equipment item, or other resource is committed to a specific activity. They are used in scheduling and planning to model partial or full allocation of available capacity.
Contrast: Resource assignment — resource units describe how much capacity is assigned, while a resource assignment identifies which resource is allocated.
Example: A business analyst is assigned at 50 percent resource units to requirements workshops while supporting another project half time.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A project schedule whose planned dates are adjusted to reflect the expected availability of required resources.
Expansion: A resource-constrained schedule reflects the fact that activities cannot always start as soon as logic alone would permit because needed resources are limited or unavailable. It is the resulting schedule after timing has been aligned to actual resource capacity. PMI treats this as the same concept as a resource-limited schedule.
Contrast: Resource leveling — resource leveling is the technique used to adjust dates for limited resources, while a resource-constrained schedule is the resulting schedule.
Example: A project delays integration testing by one week because the only qualified test engineer is committed to another critical activity.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — resource-limited schedule

Core Meaning: The scheduling practice of adjusting activity timing to fit limited resource availability.
Expansion: Resource-constrained scheduling builds a workable schedule by considering when needed resources are actually available rather than relying only on activity logic. In PMI-aligned practice, it is closely associated with resource leveling and the creation of a resource-limited schedule.
Contrast: Resource smoothing — resource-constrained scheduling may extend the project finish date to respect resource limits, while resource smoothing generally works within available float.
Example: A construction scheduler moves overlapping inspection tasks because the same certified inspector is required for each one.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A schedule that is constrained by the availability of resources needed to perform the work.
Expansion: A resource-limited schedule reflects the fact that activities can proceed only when the required people, equipment, materials, or facilities are available. It is shaped by resource constraints rather than target dates alone. In practice, this often leads to sequencing changes, delayed starts, or resource leveling so the plan remains feasible.
Contrast: Resource smoothing — a resource-limited schedule is driven by hard availability constraints; resource smoothing adjusts work within float to reduce peaks without necessarily changing the critical path.
Example: A systems implementation project pushes several testing tasks into the following week because the only certified security tester is unavailable until then.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The obligation to perform assigned project work and answer for completing that work.
Expansion: Responsibility is the duty assigned to a person or role to carry out specific project work. In PMI practice, it is used to clarify who performs tasks, produces outputs, or supports decisions within the project structure. It is especially visible in role definitions and responsibility assignment matrices, where clear ownership reduces confusion and delay.
Contrast: Accountability — responsibility is about doing the work or owning an assigned duty; accountability is about ultimate answerability for the result.
Example: In a system-implementation project, the data-migration lead is responsible for cleansing legacy data and loading it into the new platform.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — exact page citation unavailable in retrieved context

Core Meaning: A grid that shows project resources assigned to each work package.
Expansion: A responsibility assignment matrix links work packages or activities to the people or roles responsible for performing them. It is used to clarify who does what and to reduce gaps, duplication, and confusion in execution. PMI presents it as a practical way to align work definitions with assigned resources and accountability.
Contrast: RACI matrix — a responsibility assignment matrix is the broader category; a RACI matrix is one specific form that labels involvement as responsible, accountable, consulted, and informed.
Example: In an ERP implementation, the matrix shows that finance owns chart-of-accounts design, IT owns system configuration, and the vendor supports data migration tasks.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, responsibility assignment matrix

Core Meaning: A defined function assumed by or assigned to a person in a project, program, or portfolio context.
Expansion: A role describes the kind of contribution, authority, or responsibility expected from someone within a management structure. In project management, roles help clarify who makes decisions, performs work, and supports governance. PMI uses the concept to reduce ambiguity in accountability and team coordination.
Contrast: Responsibility — a role is the function or position a person fills; a responsibility is a specific duty attached to that role.
Example: In a project charter, the sponsor role is identified as the authority that approves major funding and key decisions.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The defined assignment of who does what and who answers for outcomes on a project.
Expansion: Roles and responsibilities clarify the functions individuals perform and the work they own, reducing ambiguity, gaps, and overlap. A role describes a position such as tester or sponsor, while a responsibility is the specific work or decision tied to it. Tools such as a responsibility assignment matrix make these expectations explicit and support accountability.
Contrast: Accountability — responsibility concerns who performs the work, while accountability concerns the single role that must answer for the outcome.
Example: A team charter lists the product owner as responsible for prioritising the backlog and the Scrum Master as responsible for removing impediments.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Plan Resource Management

Core Meaning: The condition of being protected from harm or risk of injury in project work or project deliverables.
Expansion: Safety is a project consideration focused on preventing harm to people, property, and the environment. It influences planning, risk responses, compliance measures, and execution controls, especially in physical delivery environments.
Contrast: Security — safety is about protection from accidental harm or injury; security is about protection from intentional threats or unauthorized access.
Example: A refinery turnaround project includes lockout-tagout procedures and protective equipment requirements to reduce risk of worker injury.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The acquisition and assignment of people with the needed skills to perform project work.
Expansion: Staffing concerns obtaining the human resources required for project activities and placing them into appropriate roles. It includes securing availability, aligning skills to work, and adjusting assignments as project needs change. In PMI project practice, staffing supports effective team formation and resource planning rather than standing alone as a formal glossary term in every standard.
Contrast: Acquire Resources — Staffing is the broader practical act of getting people into roles; Acquire Resources is the formal PMI process for obtaining team members and physical resources.
Example: A systems-integration project staffs a solution architect, two developers, a tester, and a change manager based on the work package skill needs.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A person with specialized knowledge in a discipline, function, technology, market, or process who provides expertise to support project work and decisions.
Expansion: A subject matter expert contributes deep domain knowledge that the project team may not possess internally. SMEs help refine requirements, assess options, validate deliverables, and reduce technical or business uncertainty. They may support planning, execution, quality review, risk analysis, or stakeholder engagement depending on the project's needs.
Contrast: Project team member — a subject matter expert is defined by specialized expertise contributed to the project, while a project team member may have broader delivery responsibilities without being the recognized authority in a domain.
Example: On a healthcare software project, a clinical coding expert reviews workflow requirements and confirms that the proposed design aligns with regulatory and operational practice.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, pages 43 and 82

Core Meaning: The process of improving team members' competencies, interaction, and overall team environment to enhance project performance.
Expansion: Team development focuses on strengthening how people work together as well as improving individual capability. In PMI project management practice, it includes actions that build trust, clarify collaboration, improve communication, and raise competence over time. Its purpose is to help the team perform more effectively and sustain productive working relationships throughout the project.
Contrast: Manage Team — Team development is about improving capability and team effectiveness; Manage Team is the broader process of tracking performance, resolving issues, and taking corrective action.
Example: A project manager introduces peer learning sessions, team working agreements, and retrospective actions so a newly formed cross-functional team can collaborate more effectively during delivery.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition / not enough verified page detail available from retrieval

Core Meaning: The part of the resource management plan covering how and when team members are acquired, managed, and released.
Expansion: Sometimes called the staffing management plan, this component describes how human resources will be brought onto the project, their roles and timing, training and recognition approaches, and how they will be released when no longer needed. It helps ensure the right people are available at the right time and that resource costs end when work ends. It is part of broader resource planning rather than a standalone plan.
Contrast: Team charter - a team charter sets the team's ground rules and values, whereas the team management plan addresses staffing logistics and timing.
Example: A plan showing testers join in month three, ramp to full time during system testing, and roll off after acceptance.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Evaluations of a team's effectiveness as a unit, used to improve how it works together.
Expansion: Team performance assessments are the project manager's appraisal of how well the team functions together, looking at indicators such as improved skills, lower staff turnover, and greater cohesion. They are an output of developing the team and inform actions to strengthen collaboration. They focus on the team as a whole rather than on individual appraisals.
Contrast: Performance Reviews - individual performance reviews assess a single member's work, whereas team performance assessments gauge the effectiveness of the team as a unit.
Example: After team-building, the PM notes faster decision-making and lower turnover as evidence of improved team performance.
Source: PMBOK Guide - team performance assessments

Core Meaning: The human resources assigned to or needed by the project team to perform project work and produce deliverables.
Expansion: Team resources refers to the people who carry out project activities, including project team members and, depending on context, subject matter experts and support roles. In PMI usage, this concept is central to estimating resource needs, acquiring people, developing the team, and managing performance. It connects planning decisions about skills, availability, roles, and responsibilities to execution outcomes.
Contrast: Physical resources — team resources are people and their capabilities; physical resources are equipment, materials, facilities, and infrastructure.
Example: For a construction project, the manager plans team resources such as site engineers, schedulers, safety specialists, procurement staff, and supervisors needed at different phases.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Project Resource Management chapter and glossary; exact page citation should be verified against the edition in use

Core Meaning: The way project team roles, responsibilities, reporting relationships, and collaboration patterns are arranged to deliver the work.
Expansion: Team structure describes how a project team is organised, including role design, authority distribution, reporting lines, and how different disciplines work together. In PMI practice, team structure affects decision speed, communication flow, accountability, and the team's ability to respond to change. It is shaped by the delivery approach, organisational context, and the complexity of the work, so the right structure varies by project. Good team structure supports clarity and coordination without creating unnecessary handoffs or bottlenecks.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Team structure is the design of the team as a working system, not just a list of names and titles. It defines who is involved, how responsibilities are grouped, how authority is shared, and how work moves across the team.

Why it matters and where it applies: Structure influences how quickly issues are resolved, how information flows, and whether specialists can work as an integrated team. It matters in all projects, but especially in matrix organisations, agile product delivery, vendor-heavy efforts, and complex programmes where unclear interfaces create delay and rework.

The most common misconception: that team structure is only an HR or org-chart issue. In projects, it is also a delivery design choice that directly shapes collaboration, responsiveness, and execution risk.

How it shows up in real projects: selecting a cross-functional team instead of siloed functional support, defining product owner and scrum master responsibilities, setting up integrated design-and-build squads, or creating clear workstream leads in a large transformation.
Contrast: Organisational structure — organisational structure describes the wider enterprise arrangement of authority and reporting; team structure describes how the project team itself is set up to do the work.
Example: For a digital transformation project, the sponsor creates a cross-functional team with business, architecture, development, testing, and change leads who work through a single integrated backlog rather than separate department queues.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The person responsible for guiding the technical direction and quality of a project's solution.
Expansion: A technical lead owns architectural and engineering decisions, mentors the team on technical matters, and ensures the solution meets technical requirements. The role bridges project management and hands-on delivery without necessarily holding formal people-management authority.
Contrast: Distinct from the project manager, who owns scope, schedule, and stakeholders rather than the technical design.
Example: The technical lead chooses the system architecture and reviews critical code while the project manager tracks the plan.
Source: Common project role; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A planned activity to build the knowledge and skills people need to perform project work or use a delivered solution effectively.
Expansion: Training prepares team members, users, or operators to carry out roles, adopt new processes, or use a product or system correctly. In project management practice it commonly supports transition, readiness, change adoption, and sustainment rather than existing as an end in itself. It reduces implementation risk by improving capability and helping the delivered result achieve its intended benefits.
Contrast: Coaching — training provides structured instruction for specific knowledge or skills; coaching supports ongoing performance improvement through guidance.
Example: Before a new customer-support platform goes live, the project team trains service agents on the system workflow, escalation paths, and reporting features.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The deliberate development of new or stronger capabilities so people can perform current or emerging project work effectively.
Expansion: Upskilling refers to strengthening existing team or organizational capabilities so work can be performed at the needed level of competence. In project environments it is often tied to new delivery approaches, technologies, regulatory demands, or strategic priorities. It supports capability building, reduces execution risk, and helps organisations sustain change after delivery.
Contrast: Training — upskilling is the broader capability-building outcome, while training is one method used to achieve it.
Example: Before launching an AI-enabled product initiative, the organisation upskills project staff in data governance, agile delivery, and model risk oversight.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The extent to which a resource's available capacity is being used for assigned work.
Expansion: Utilization describes how much of a resource's available time or capability is committed to work. It is used in resource planning and control to understand workload, efficiency, and capacity constraints. While higher utilization can indicate strong use of resources, excessive utilization reduces flexibility and can create bottlenecks, delays, and burnout.
Contrast: Availability — utilization is the share of capacity already being used, while availability is the capacity still open for assignment.
Example: A portfolio manager tracks a specialist at high utilization across several projects and delays new assignments to avoid creating a scheduling bottleneck.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A dedicated shared space where a project team co-locates to collaborate intensively.
Expansion: A war room is a central physical space where team members work together, post information radiators, and make rapid decisions. Co-locating in this way improves communication, builds team identity, and speeds problem solving. The concept is also described as a tight matrix when used to gather a dispersed team.
Contrast: Unlike distributed or virtual teams that rely on digital tools, a war room concentrates people in one place to maximize face-to-face interaction.
Example: The launch team takes over a conference room as a war room for the final two weeks before go-live.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The periods, defined by a calendar, during which resources are available to perform project work.
Expansion: Working time is set through project and resource calendars that specify workdays, shifts, holidays, and non-working periods. Scheduling tools use it to convert activity durations into calendar dates and to calculate resource availability.
Contrast: Differs from elapsed time, which counts all calendar time including non-working periods.
Example: Because the resource calendar shows no working time on weekends, a five-day task starting Thursday finishes the following Wednesday.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The amount of project work assigned to a person, team, or other resource over a defined period.
Expansion: Workload reflects how much effort or how many assignments a resource is expected to handle relative to available time and capacity. It is important in resource planning because excessive workload creates delays, multitasking inefficiency, and burnout, while too little workload leaves capacity unused. In PMI practice, the idea is handled through resource allocation, capacity, and overallocation management even when the term itself is used informally.
Contrast: Capacity — workload is the work assigned; capacity is the work a resource can realistically perform.
Example: A project manager sees that one business analyst is assigned to requirements work on three projects in the same week and redistributes tasks to avoid overallocation.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry
